# Pregnancy & lactation

> Pregnancy remodels a woman's body on its own — expanding her blood, retuning her insulin,
> doubling how much calcium she absorbs. The clinician's job is not to micromanage that machinery
> but to know the few things that genuinely matter, counsel them well, and recognise when to refer.

## Pregnancy at a glance: two drivers

Almost everything in this chapter can be read through one lens. Pregnancy changes a woman's
nutrition through **internal drivers** — the automatic physiology of placental hormones, an
expanding blood volume, a deliberate slide into insulin resistance, a gut that suddenly absorbs
calcium twice as well — set against **external levers** the woman and her clinician can actually
pull: what she eats, how she moves, the screening she is offered, and the foods she avoids.

Hold that pairing and the counter-intuitive facts stop being surprising: calcium needs barely rise
*because* absorption doubles; a mid-pregnancy haemoglobin falls *because* plasma outpaces red cells;
gestational diabetes appears *because* a normal insulin-resistant adaptation outruns the pancreas.
The dial below lets you watch the requirements move — and spot the ones that don't.

<div data-island="requirement-dial" data-src="/diagrams/requirement-dial.yml"></div>

## Before pregnancy: the real starting line

The most important nutrition window opens **before conception**. The neural tube closes by about
**28 days** — often before a woman knows she is pregnant — so **folic acid has to be running
already**: ~400 µg/day for most, but **4–5 mg/day** for higher-risk women (a previous
neural-tube-defect pregnancy, diabetes, obesity, or certain anticonvulsants) [@mrc1991folate;
@czeizel1992folate]. The same window is where weight and blood-glucose are best optimised and where
teratogenic supplements (high-dose vitamin A) are stopped [@johnson2006preconception]. Much of good
antenatal nutrition is really *pre*-natal.

## Energy: "eating for two" is a myth — until lactation

The oldest myth is that pregnancy doubles a woman's food needs. In fact the energy cost is
**modest and back-loaded**: roughly **nothing extra** in the first trimester, about **+340 kcal/day**
in the second and **+450 kcal/day** in the third — and measured expenditure often rises even less
than that [@savard2020energy]. It is one extra snack, not a second dinner.

**Lactation is the one stage where the phrase nearly earns its keep.** Making milk costs ~670 kcal a
day; about 170 come from the fat a woman laid down in pregnancy, leaving a *net* extra requirement of
roughly **450–500 kcal/day**, with more fluid too [@faoEnergy2004]. Pregnancy fat gain, in other
words, is the biological down-payment for breastfeeding.

**Weight gain is a target, not a free-for-all.** Healthy gain depends on pre-pregnancy BMI
(underweight ~12.5–18 kg, normal ~11.5–16, overweight ~7–11.5, obese ~5–9) [@iom2009gwg], and the
risk is **U-shaped**: too little raises the chance of a small or preterm baby, too much of a large
baby and a caesarean — yet only about a third of women land in range [@goldstein2017gwg]. Those
ranges rest on Western BMI cut-offs and may fit local women imperfectly, which Singapore data bear
out [@ee2014gwgSG; @he2019gwgSG] — so treat them as a framework, not a verdict.

**Activity is the clean external lever.** For a woman without obstetric contraindications, about
**150 minutes a week** of moderate activity lowers the risk of gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia
and caesarean, and helps keep weight gain in range; a previously active woman can generally carry on
[@acog2020exercise].

## The blood changes: dilution, not always deficiency

A mid-pregnancy haemoglobin almost always falls — and it is usually **not** anaemia. Plasma volume
expands by nearly **half**, outpacing the ~25% rise in red-cell mass, so the haemoglobin drops by
**dilution** [@aguree2019plasma]. The lever is to use **pregnancy-specific cut-offs** and check
**ferritin** before treating, rather than reflexively iron-loading every low number.

That said, true **iron deficiency** is genuinely common — even here: about **three-quarters** of
Singaporean women are iron-deficient by the early third trimester [@loy2019ironSG]. Requirements rise
for the expanding red-cell mass and the fetus's own stores, and daily oral iron (WHO: 30–60 mg with
folic acid) reduces maternal anaemia and low birth weight [@penarosas2015iron]. The nutrient basics
sit in the *Micronutrients, Hydration & Electrolytes* chapter; here the practical point is
**adherence**, because iron's gut side-effects are where supplementation quietly fails:

- Take it with a **vitamin-C source**, and **away from tea, coffee and calcium**.
- Consider **alternate-day dosing** — single doses every other day are absorbed *better* than daily
  divided ones and cause fewer gut symptoms [@stoffel2017Iron].
- Expect **constipation** (progesterone already slows the gut, and iron compounds it); more fibre and
  fluid are first-line [@rungsiprakarn2015constipation].

## Early pregnancy: nausea, and the red flag behind it

Nausea and vomiting affect most pregnancies in the first trimester — the flip side of "no extra
energy yet." Small, frequent, bland meals help, and **ginger** and **vitamin B6 (pyridoxine)** are
reasonable first-line measures [@acog2018nvp]. Most of it is miserable but benign.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do no harm: hyperemesis and the thiamine rule
When vomiting is severe and persistent — with **weight loss, dehydration and ketosis** — it is
**hyperemesis gravidarum**, and it needs referral, not reassurance. The safety point to carry: a
woman who has been vomiting for weeks may be **thiamine-depleted**, and giving glucose or dextrose
*first* can precipitate **Wernicke's encephalopathy**. **Thiamine goes in before any glucose**
[@rcogHyperemesis69].
:::

## The micronutrients that matter — and the one that harms

Beyond folate and iron, a short list carries most of the weight:

- **Iodine** — the requirement rises to about **250 µg/day** for the fetal thyroid hormone that
  builds the brain; the early fetus depends entirely on the mother [@bath2013iodine]. Singapore's
  salt iodisation is **voluntary**, so status can't be assumed — iodised salt is a sensible default
  [@codling2017iodine].
- **Calcium** — the surprise: the requirement **doesn't really rise**, because the gut roughly
  **doubles** its absorption in pregnancy [@kovacs2016mineral]. Ensure an adequate baseline, no more.
- **Vitamin D** — supplementation *probably* lowers pre-eclampsia and gestational diabetes, but the
  evidence was recently downgraded, so counsel it modestly; deficiency is common locally despite the
  sun [@palacios2024vitD; @sgVitDeficiency].
- **Choline and DHA** — the under-taught pair. Most pregnant women fall short of choline; and marine
  **omega-3 (DHA)** modestly reduces preterm birth [@middleton2018omega3; @caudill2018choline].
- **Vitamin A — the "excess harms" nutrient.** High-dose *preformed* retinol (supplements, liver) is
  **teratogenic**; the **β-carotene** in plants is safe [@rothman1995vitA]. This is the one nutrient
  where the counsel is a **ceiling**, not a floor — check that a multivitamin isn't over-dosed and
  skip the liver.

::: {.callout-tip}
## 🍜 Eating out & 🍳 healthy kitchen: safety, and the fish paradox
Pregnancy adds a food-safety layer: skip **raw and undercooked** items (sashimi, raw oysters,
half-boiled or raw egg — including raw egg stirred into porridge), cold **deli meats** and
**unpasteurised** dairy (listeria), and limit the big predators (**shark, swordfish, marlin**,
and tuna sashimi) for mercury. But don't over-correct into avoiding fish altogether — low seafood
intake tracks with *worse* child neurodevelopment [@hibbeln2007seafood]. Eat **2–3 servings a week of
low-mercury fish** instead. And keep the everyday drinks in view for caffeine: kopi, teh, Milo and
bubble tea all count toward the daily cap.
:::

## Gestational diabetes: why Singapore screens everyone

Gestational diabetes is Singapore's defining antenatal nutrition issue, and it follows straight from
the two-driver lens. **Internally**, insulin sensitivity falls by **50–60%** (the body's cells respond less
to insulin) in the third trimester
of *every* pregnancy — a normal adaptation, driven by a mix of placental and maternal hormones (no
single one is the culprit), that diverts glucose to the growing fetus [@kampmann2019ir]. GDM is what
happens when the pancreas **can't compensate** for that resistance [@plows2018gdm] — which is exactly
why it forecasts a woman's future diabetes.

**Externally**, the lever is *how* we screen. The local **GUSTO** cohort found GDM in roughly **one
in five** pregnancies, and — load-bearingly — that screening only the "high-risk" women would have
**missed about half** of cases, because risk factors predict GDM poorly in Asian women [@gusto_gdm].
So Singapore screens **universally**: a **75 g glucose tolerance test at 24–28 weeks** for every
pregnancy [@aceGDM2018]. And there is no natural cut-point to find — the landmark **HAPO** study
showed glucose and outcomes rise on a smooth continuum, so the diagnostic thresholds are a **chosen
line**, not a biological cliff [@hapo2008].

::: {.callout-note}
## The chain, not the numbers
The thresholds (fasting ≥5.1, 1-hour ≥10.0, 2-hour ≥8.5 mmol/L) aren't for memorising — the *pathway*
is. Maternal glucose crosses to the fetus, whose pancreas answers with its own insulin; that drives
**overgrowth (macrosomia)** before birth and, once the maternal glucose supply is cut at delivery,
leaves the baby briefly **hypoglycaemic**. And the mother carries a roughly **seven-fold** future
risk of type 2 diabetes [@bellamy2009] — so she needs a **postpartum glucose test at 4–12 weeks** and
lifelong vigilance. That link is why GDM sits inside Singapore's *War on Diabetes* [@mohWarOnDiabetes].
:::

First-line management is **dietary** — and it works best *within* a woman's usual food. A local
"seafood-and-noodle" pattern was actually protective [@gustoDietGDM], which licenses **familiar
swaps** — brown rice or wholegrain *bee hoon* for white rice, water or a Nutri-Grade A/B drink for the
sweet one — over an unfamiliar regimen. Insulin and glucose targets, when diet isn't enough, belong to
the **obstetric and diabetes team by referral** (an endocrinology matter), not to a first consult.

## Lactation: the mother's side

Once the baby arrives, the nutrition question splits in two. **Infant feeding itself** — latch,
weaning, complementary foods, the infant's own vitamin D — belongs to the *Infancy & Early Childhood*
chapter. What stays here is the **mother**: her extra energy and fluid, and one elegant piece of
physiology.

The body **protects the milk** for some nutrients at the mother's own expense, but not for others.
**Buffered** nutrients — iron, calcium, folate, zinc — stay steady in milk even if she runs low (she
pays, not the baby). **Diet-dependent** nutrients — **vitamin B12, iodine, vitamin D, DHA** — fall if
*she* is deficient (the baby pays) [@dgac2020lactation]. The danger case is the **vegan or strict
vegetarian mother**: she can look perfectly well while producing B12-critically-low milk that harms
the infant's developing brain [@kuhne1991b12] — so B12 supplementation and a dietitian referral are
the move. (Milk is naturally low in vitamin D whatever the mother eats, which is why the *infant* is
supplemented — the dose is the infancy chapter's.)

Two quick myths to retire: **alcohol** passes into milk and only *time* clears it — "pump and dump"
relieves fullness, not alcohol — so feed before a drink or wait; and **caffeine** up to ~200–300 mg a
day is compatible. And the "milk-boosting" soups and herbs? The evidence that any galactagogue raises
supply is **weak**; frequent, effective milk removal is what actually drives production
[@foong2020galactagogue]. Support the practice warmly, but don't oversell it.

## The Singapore picture: confinement

The postpartum **confinement month** is near-universal here and patterned by community — Chinese
*zuo yue zi*, Malay *berpantang*, Indian practices — resting on a shared "heaty/cooling" food model,
and it is a genuine, structured dietary pattern with real links to maternal mood [@fok2016confinement;
@teo2018confinement]. The competency is **respectful negotiation, not correction**: support the large
harmless majority, and guide only the genuinely risky edges — **alcohol** cooked into rice-wine dishes
(it reaches the milk), a heavy **sodium and fat** load, and the wholesale avoidance of "cooling"
**fruit and vegetables** (the very source of vitamin C, fibre and folate). The same respectful lens
covers the vegetarian mother (plan B12 and iron) and the halal family (a supplement-sourcing question,
not a deficiency).

## So — does a pregnant woman eat for two?

No — and saying so does harm. In pregnancy the win is **quality, not quantity**: folate started
early, enough iron and iodine, a sensible weight-gain target, a short list of foods to avoid, and the
one screening test that catches a common, hidden problem. It is **lactation** that finally asks for
real extra energy. The skill this chapter builds is telling the few things that genuinely matter from
the many that don't — and knowing, at every step, when the answer is not a diet but a **referral**.

## Check your understanding

<div data-island="quiz" data-src="/quizzes/pregnancy-lactation.quiz.yml"></div>

## Practise: Mrs Devi, 26 weeks pregnant

Mrs Devi feels well and wonders whether she really needs "that glucose test." Work through her
pregnancy — the myths, the blood count, the screening no one should skip, and where your role ends
and the team's begins. Your choices branch the case.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/pregnancy-gdm.case.yml"></div>
